coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of bright yellow fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and their applications to an off–on probe for highly sensitive detection of methimazole
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Chunhe
Qin, Dongmiao
Jiang, Xiaohua
Zheng, Xiangfei
Deng, Biyang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, bright yellow fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (YN-CQDs) are produced by a facile, inexpensive and eco-friendly hydrothermal technique using extract of chlorophyll from lettuce leaf as precursor. The quantum yield (QY) of as-synthesized YN-CQDs was up to 38%, which was higher than the previously reported quantum yield of most CQDs prepared using other biomass. Silver ion (Ag+) can covalently bond with carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of YN-CQDs and quench the fluorescence of YN-CQDs through static quenching. Upon addition of methimazole (MZ) to the YN-CQDs/Ag+ dispersion system, Ag+ releases from the YN-CQDs/Ag+ complex due to the high affinity of MZ for Ag+ and the quenched fluorescence of the YN-CQDs was recovered. Hence, we developed an off–on sensing method for the detection of MZ using YN-CQDs/Ag+ as a fluorescent probe. Under optimal conditions, with addition of MZ, the fluorescence recovery of YN-CQDs/Ag+ system happens with a good linearity in the range of 0.003 ~ 60 μmol/L and a detection limit of 0.8 nmol/L. The YN-CQDs/Ag+ system exhibited good MZ determination performance. The established off–on fluorescence sensing platform was successfully employed for detecting MZ in mouse plasma.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Lettuce leaf
Methimazole
Fluorescent probe
Mouse plasma

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism for the fluorescence response of YN-CQDs to MZ
Detection of MZ in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21765004 ) and by the Guangxi Science Foundation of China ( 2019GXNSFAA245076 ). The research fund of State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources (Guangxi Normal University) ( CMEMR2018-C18 ) is gratefully acknowledged.

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21765004) and by the Guangxi Science Foundation of China (2019GXNSFAA245076). The research fund of State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources (Guangxi Normal University) (CMEMR2018-C18) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence spectra of YN-CQDs, YN-CQDs/Ag+, YN-CQDs/Ag+/MZ, Ag+/MZ, and YN-CQDs/MZ. (b) Fluorescence decay traces of YN-CQDs, YN-CQDs/Ag+, and YN-CQDs/Ag+/MZ. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of YN-CQDs, YN-CQDs/Ag+, and YN-CQDs/Ag+/MZ. (d) Size distributions of the YN-CQDs (red line), YN-CQDs/Ag+ (blue line), and YN-CQDs/Ag+/MZ (black line). (e) Zeta potential distributions and Zeta potential values of the YN-CQDs, YN-CQDs/Ag+, and YN-CQDs/Ag+/MZ. YN-CQDs: 8 μg/mL, Ag+: 100 μmol/L, MZ: 60 μmol/L.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the YN-CQDs/Ag+ system upon addition of various concentrations of MZ. (b) Relationship between recovery efficiency and the concentration of MZ. Inset: a linear relationship of recovery efficiency versus the concentration of MZ over the range from 0.003 to 60 μmol/L (λex = 420 nm). The concentrations of YN-CQDs and Ag+ are 8 μg/mL and 100 μmol/L, respectively.
Selectivity of the sensor. The concentrations of MZ and YN-CQDs are 60 μmol/L and 8 μg/mL, respectively. The possible interfering substance concentration is 100 μmol/L each.
Schematic illustration of the formation of YN-CQDs and the detection of MZ based on the off–on strategy.
Analytical results of MZ in mouse plasma (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of bright yellow fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and their applications to an off–on probe for highly sensitive detection of methimazole

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources
School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, bright yellow fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (YN-CQDs) are produced by a facile, inexpensive and eco-friendly hydrothermal technique using extract of chlorophyll from lettuce leaf as precursor. The quantum yield (QY) of as-synthesized YN-CQDs was up to 38%, which was higher than the previously reported quantum yield of most CQDs prepared using other biomass. Silver ion (Ag+) can covalently bond with carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of YN-CQDs and quench the fluorescence of YN-CQDs through static quenching. Upon addition of methimazole (MZ) to the YN-CQDs/Ag+ dispersion system, Ag+ releases from the YN-CQDs/Ag+ complex due to the high affinity of MZ for Ag+ and the quenched fluorescence of the YN-CQDs was recovered. Hence, we developed an off–on sensing method for the detection of MZ using YN-CQDs/Ag+ as a fluorescent probe. Under optimal conditions, with addition of MZ, the fluorescence recovery of YN-CQDs/Ag+ system happens with a good linearity in the range of 0.003 ~ 60 μmol/L and a detection limit of 0.8 nmol/L. The YN-CQDs/Ag+ system exhibited good MZ determination performance. The established off–on fluorescence sensing platform was successfully employed for detecting MZ in mouse plasma.

ce:section.ce:para: Methimazole (1-methyl-2-mercaptoimidazole, MZ) is one of the most commonly used drugs for the treatment of human hyperthyroidism [1]. It is a thioamide that reduces the synthesis of thyroid hormone by interdicting the activity of thyroid peroxidase and iodination of the tyrosine residues in thyroglobulin [2,3]. But an excessive MZ dose can cause adverse reactions in human body including skin irritation, arthralgia, impaired taste, agranulocytosis, and rare cases of nephritis and liver cirrhosis [4–7]. Hence, it is necessary to establish an effective and fast response approach to monitor MZ in clinical chemistry, pharmaceutical as well as other fields. To date, several analytical techniques have been employed to monitor MZ, including liquid chromatography [8], liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC/MS) [7], voltammetry [9], spectrophotometry [10,11], and chemiluminescence [12,13]. Although these methods have high accuracy and selectivity, most of these usually require high instrument cost, time-consuming operation procedures and complex sample pretreatment procedures, which limits their use in analysis of the actual samples. Based on this consideration, it is of great significance to developa relatively economical, good selectivity, and outstanding sensitivity method for identificationof the level of MZ. At this point, with the distinct advantages like fast response, easy operation, low analysis cost, and superior selectivity and sensitivity, fluorescence method has attracted wide attention. In recent years, a variety offluorescent materials, including semiconductor quantum dots [14], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [15], noble metal (silver, gold, copper) nanoclusters [16–18], and organic dyes [19] have been widely used in the field of fluorescence sensing. However, most fluorescence sensors have disadvantages such as large size, high cost or complicated preparation process. Notably, MZ has a very strong binding ability to Ag+ and can capture Ag+ from the sensor and Ag+ complex [20]. So, further establishing a cost-effective, sensitive and fast response fluorescence “off–on” approach for detecting MZ is of important concern.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have become an ideal candidate for biological labeling, drug delivery, and fluorescent sensing because of their convenient preparation and functionalization, excellent photostability, very low toxicity, excellent aqueous solubility, adjustable photoluminescence, and favorable biocompatibility [21–24]. At present, CQDs have been widely applied in environmental, chemical, and biological field including heavy metals, free radicals, bioenzymes, amino acids, pH, and so on [25–27]. Although many CQDs have been reported, their emissions are mostly concentrated in the blue to green light regions, which limits their further applications in biomedical and optical optoelectronic devices. Therefore, the development of CQDs with yellow or red fluorescence emission could be highly valuable. Recently, surface functionalization and heteroatom doping were promising methods for improving the optical properties of CQDs like quantum yield and long wavelength emission [28]. Nevertheless, surface functionalization usually involves some drawbacks such as time-consuming preparation processes, the use of toxic reagent, and some environmental factors, such as fluorescence instability caused by pH value [29]. In addition, in the reported heteroatom-doped CQDs, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) show outstanding physicochemical properties, which made their valid utilization in sensing, catalysis and cellular imaging fields [30,31]. Generally, top-down and bottom-up methods have been adopted for preparing N-CQDs [30]. The top-down methods are mainly to fabricate N-CQDs via cutting and decomposing of different carbon materials, such as arc discharge carbon nanotubes [32], chemical oxidation of candle soot [33], laser ablated graphite [34] or graphene nanocrystals [35], and electrochemical graphite oxide rod [36]. However, the QY of N-CQDs obtained by these methods is low and the preparation process is complex. Therefore, the bottom-up method is widely used to prepare N-CQDs, mainly through microwave pyrolysis or hydrothermal treatment of nitrogen-rich precursors. Although N-CQDs prepared by this method have a high QY, they usually emit strong fluorescenceonly in the blue regionunder excitation and require a large number of oxidants in the preparation process, which restricts their application in the biological fields [37]. Therefore, establishing a simple, cost-effective and green way for producing N-CQDs with long wavelength emission remains indeed highly desirable.

Herein, we report a facile, green, and low-cost hydrothermal method for the synthesis of N-CQDs emitted bright yellow fluorescence (YN-CQDs) by using extract of chlorophyll from lettuce leaves as the precursor for the first time, without any toxic agents or surface passivation chemicals (Scheme 1 ). We found that the carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of YN-CQDs could bind to Ag+, and the fluorescence of YN-CQDs quenching happens with the addition of Ag+. Interestingly, in the presence of MZ, the quenched fluorescence of the YN-CQDs/Ag+ system was recovered because Ag+ and MZ could form a more stable Ag+−MZ complex. Subsequently, we further confirmed that YN-CQDs as an off–on fluorescent probe has been applied for recognition of MZ with high sensitivity and selectivity. Moreover, the fluorescent probe based on YN-CQDs has been also extended to detect MZ in actual samples.

HgCl2, KCl, NaCl, FeCl3·6H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, ZnCl2, CaCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, Pb(NO3)2, MnCl2, CoCl2·6H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, CdCl2, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, AgCl, NaBr were acquired from Guangdong Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. Glucose, sucrose, urea, ascorbic acid, L-cysteine (L-Cys), and human serum albumin were bought from Shanghai Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the mentioned above reagents were of analytical grade and directly utilized without any other treatment before using. Double-distilled water (DDW) was used all the experiment.

RF-5301 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was utilized to obtain the fluorescent (FL) spectra of YN-CQDs. Likewise, the UV absorption spectra of YN-CQDs were acquired by Cary 60 spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA). A PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrophotometer from Perkin-Elmer (USA) was employed to identify theFourier transform infrared spectroscopy(FT-IR) of YN-CQDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of YN-CQDs were characterized using a JEOL-JEM-2100F transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). The FL3-P-TCSPC time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, Longjumeau, France) was performed to acquire the time-resolved decay curves of YN-CQDs. An ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used for X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. The structure and crystallinity of YN-CQDs were characterized by an X-ray powder diffractometer (D/max-2500 V/PC, Rigaku, Japan).

For the synthesis of YN-CQDs, 100 g of lettuce leaves and 70 mL of ethanolwere placed in an electrical juicing machine and processed into juice. Then, acetone (140 mL) was introduced to the above mixture and extracted for 40 min, and filtered with qualitative filter paper. Next, the filtrate was completely shifted to a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) to complete the reactionat 180 °C for 6 h, after which a brown solution was obtained upon cooling to room temperature. Subsequently, the resulting YN-CQDs solution through centrifuge (11,000 rpm) for 15 min and filtrated with a 0.22 μm syringe filters to remove large particles. To obtain pure YN-CQDs, the resultant solution was dialyzed through a dialysis bag (MWCO = 1000 Da) for 48 h. Last, the resulting YN-CQDs solution was conserved at 4 °C for later use.

10 μL of YN-CQDs solution (8 μg/mL) were mixed with 100 μL of 100 μmol/L Ag+ solution and reacted in centrifuge tube for one minute. MZ (100 μL) with various concentrations were introduced with the previous prepared YN-CQDs and Ag+ mixture. Then these mixtures were dissolved with 10 mmol/L PBS (pH = 7.4) and the final volume was made up to 500 μL. Then, after all the mixtures were incubated for 1 min at room temperature, the fluorescence measurement (Ex = 420 nm, Em = 559 nm) was recorded.

The mice were forbidden to eat for 12 h before taking samples, but were allowed to drink water freely. The experimental mice were given the drug orally of 5 mg MZ/kg weight. Mice in the control group were fed the identical amount of DDW. Blood samples (1 mL) were taken from the venous plexus of mice two hours after oral administration of the drug and were placed in heparin sodium-treated centrifugal test tubes. The treatment of all blood samples was based on the previous reference [38]. In order to acquire plasma sample, the above samples were centrifuged at 3500 rpm for 10 min. Plasma sample of 200 μL was put into a centrifuge tube, and then 0.1 mol/L NaOH (50 μL) and ethyl acetate (1 mL) were added into the tube. The mixture was then whirlpool oscillated for 5 min and was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min. The upper ethyl acetate was decanted into another centrifuge tube. The bottom slag was extracted twice with ethyl acetate. Then, the extract was evaporated to dry under a stream of dry nitrogen at 80 °C. The obtained dry slag was dissolved in DDW (200 μL) for MZ measurement.

The morphology and structure of YN-CQDs were investigated using TEM. As shown in Fig. S1a, the as-prepared YN-CQDs dispersed uniformly in a spherical shape. The size distribution histogram of YN-CQDs was obtained by counting about 100 particles. As shown in Fig. S1b, the particle size distribution of YN-CQDs ranges from 2.1 to 3.9 nm with a mean particle size of 2.8 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in the inset of Fig. S1a demonstrates that lattice spacing of YN-CQDs is 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the (100) diffraction facets of graphite carbon [39]. Like most reported CQDs, the powder XRD pattern of YN-CQDs (Fig. S1c) displayed a broad diffraction peak centered at 25°, indicating graphitic structure of the synthesized YN-CQDs.

Surface functional groups and components of YN-CQDs were identified by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S1d, the broad absorption band at 3100–3400 cm−1 could belong to the stretching vibration of –OH/N–H. The band at 1622 cm−1 aroused from the stretching vibration of CO and the strong absorption band at 1500 cm−1 was identified as stretching vibration of CC. The peak at 1380 cm−1 was due to stretching vibration of C-N, whereas peak at 1280 cm−1 was the result of the C-O/C-N bond. The above results reveal that there is abundanthydroxyl, carboxylic and amino groups on the surface of YN-CQDs, which make YN-CQDs becoming hydrophilic.

We performed XPS measurements to further understand surface structure of YN-CQDs. The XPS full scan spectrum presented in Fig. S2a shows three typical peaks at 284 eV, 401.2 eV, and 531.8 eV, corresponding to C1s (64.46%), N1s (7.64%), and O1s (27.9%), respectively, indicating that YN-CQDs were a carbon frameworks composed of carbon atoms and oxygen atoms doped or modified and there are abundant functional groups containing N and O on the carbon frameworks. In addition, the lower ratio of oxygen comparatively with carbon might be due to the incorporation of nitrogen into the carbon frameworks. Fig. S2b reveals the high resolution spectrum of C1s, which exhibited the distinctive five peaks of C–C, C–N, C–O, CO, and O–CO at 284.8 eV, 285.5 eV, 286.6 eV, 287.8 eV, and 288.9 eV, respectively. The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. S2c) was attributed to two contributions, corresponding to 398.5 eV for pyrrole N (C–NC), and 401.4 eV for graphite N (N-C3). Meanwhile, the two peaks at 531.3 eV and 532.6 eV in Fig. S2d are CO and C–OH bonds, respectively, which was consistent with previous work [40]. These results confirmed that the surface of YN-CQDs is rich in N and O groups, which is consistent with the FTIR spectra. These groups not only enhance the hydrophilicity of YN-CQDs, but also can be used as the surface active site of YN-CQDs, thus enhancing the fluorescence performance of YN-CQDs.

To examine the optical property of the YN-CQDs, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of YN-CQDs were analyzed at room temperature. The UV–vis absorption of YN-CQDs was shown in Fig. S3a. The spectrum illustrates a characteristic absorption band at 270 nm, which might be ascribed to the π–π* transition of CO bond. As shown in the inset of Fig. S3a, the color of YN-CQDs solution is yellowunder sun light, and YN-CQDs emit bright yellow fluorescence under irradiation of a 365 nm UV lamp. In the field of biological imaging or biomedicine, the YN-CQDs with yellow fluorescence not only have good tissue penetration but also can avoid the interference of spontaneous blue fluorescence of the biological matrix, which is favorable for practical applications of YN-CQDs. In addition, it can be seen from the fluorescence spectrum (Fig. S3a),the YN-CQDs provided optimal fluorescence emission at 559 nm under the best excitation wavelength of 420 nm. According to a number of studies, the conjugated aromatic π systems can make the emission wavelength redshift. At the same time, it can be seen from the XPS results that some N atoms will form pyrrole N (C–NC) and graphite N (N-C3) on the surface of the YN-CQDs, which can not only help YN-CQDs to form conjugated aromatic π systems more effectively but also can establish some N-related energy states and reduce the energy gap, resulting in a yellow fluorescence emission [41]. As presented in Fig. S3b, by adjusting the excitation wavelength from 320 to 500 nm, YN-CQDs exhibit an excitation independent emission, which indicated that there is a relatively uniform emission state on the surface of YN-CQDs. Notably, the QY of the YN-CQDs was calculated to be 38% with respect to quinine sulfate as a standard sample according to eqn. S1, which is higher than that of the most reported CODs synthesized using biomass as the precursor.

One of the key features for the usefulness of nanoprobes in biological application is the fluorescence stability of the nanoparticles. Then, to investigate the stability of YN-CQDs, we studied the effects of pH, ionic strength, long-term storage and illumination of 365 nm UV light on their fluorescence intensities. As demonstrated in Fig. S4a, the fluorescent signal of YN-CQDs remained steady in the broad pH range of 5–10, while the fluorescence intensity dropped significantly when the pH was lower than 5.0 or higher than 10.0. The above results indicate that the fluorescence intensity of YN-CQDs decreased under strong acidic or alkali conditions, which might be ascribed to surface charge change caused by protonation-deprotonation of the functional groups on the surface of YN-CQDs, thereby suppressing the fluorescence. Meanwhile, Fig. S4b depicts that the fluorescence intensity of YN-CQDs has no significant changes though the concentration of NaCl ran up to 4 mol/L. This result confirmed the promising utilization of the as-prepared YN-CQDs in physiological salt environments. In addition, the YN-CQDs remained approximately constant after 2.5 months of storage or exposure to 365 nm UV light for 180 min (Fig. S4c, S4d), suggesting that YN-CQDs possess a better bleaching resistance. All the above results indicate that YN-CQDs as a fluorescence probe have great potential for complex samples analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the fluorescence response of YN-CQDs to MZ
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the feasibility of YN-CQDs for MZ detection, the fluorescence spectra of some related systems including YN-CQDs, YN-CQDs/Ag+, YN-CQDs/Ag+/MZ, Ag+/MZ, and YN-CQDs/MZ were measured. As demonstrated in Fig. 1 a, upon introducing 100 μmol/L Ag+ into the YN-CQDs, the fluorescence intensity of YN-CQDs at 559 nm is reduced more than 80%. Ag+ can easily coordinate with hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups on the surface of YN-CQDs therefore, it changes the surface defects and exciton distribution of YN-CQDs, which results in fluorescence quenching of YN-CQDs. To further confirm the quenching mechanism, we measured the fluorescence decay curves and UV–vis spectroscopy of YN-CQDs in the presence and absence of Ag+. Fig. 1b shows the average lifetime of YN-CQDs is 1.94 ns, while after adding Ag+ to YN-CQDs, the average lifetime is 1.93 ns. The average lifetime of YN-CQDs in the absence and presence of Ag+ was almost unchanged, which proves that the quenching mechanism between the YN-CQDs and Ag+ is a static quenching, therefore, the fluorescence quenching of YN-CQDs may be due to the formation of a complex between YN-CQDs and Ag+. In addition, as shown in Fig. 1c, the absorption peak shifts to the longer wavelength with the introduction of Ag+, which further confirms the formation of metal complexes between Ag+ and carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of YN-CQDs.

Since Ag+ has a great affinity towards MZ, we employ YN-CQDs/Ag+ system as a fluorescence probe for sensing MZ. As shown in Fig. 1a, the quenched fluorescence of the YN-CQDs/Ag+ system was significantly restored in the presence of MZ. In a control experiment, no fluorescence was observed when MZ was incubated with the Ag+ solution alone. Additionally, when the MZ solution was added to the YN-CQDs dispersion alone, there is no change in fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, the average fluorescence lifetime of YN-CQDs/Ag+/MZ mixture was 1.94 ns (Fig. 1b), which was consistent with the average fluorescence lifetime of YN-CQDs dispersion alone (1.94 ns). These results indicate that Ag+ has higher affinity for MZ than for YN-CQDs, which caused release of Ag+ from the surface of YN-CQDs and recovery of the fluorescence. To further understand the interaction mechanism, dynamiclightscattering and zeta potential were measured to monitor the surface change of YN-CQDs during in response to Ag+ and MZ, the results appear in Fig. 1d and e. The average diameter of YN-CQDs/Ag+ is 459 nm, which is much higher than that of YN-CQDs (91.3 nm) (see Fig. 1d). This further suggests that the complex of YN-CQDs and Ag+ results in the increase ofthe average diameter. When MZ was introduced into YN-CQDs/Ag+ system, Ag+ was released from the surface of YN-CQDs and the average diameter (122 nm) is similar tothe average diameter of YN-CQDs. The zeta potential also provided the consistent results (Fig. 1e). The zeta potential of YN-CQDs aqueous solution increased dramatically from −16 mV to + 1.85 mV in the presence of Ag+, probably owing to the consumption of the carboxyl or other negative groups on the surface of YN-CQDs with the introduction of Ag+ and it further confirms that the successful combination of Ag+ with YN-CQDs. While when MZ was added to the YN-CQDs/Ag+ system solution, the zeta potential was −13.9 mV. These results showed that Ag+ binding with MZ resulted in the formation of Ag+/MZ complex, which leads to the fluorescence recovery of YN-CQDs.

In order to obtain the good fluorescent assay responding toward MZ, the Ag+ concentration, pH value, and equilibrium time were optimized. The fluorescence response of YN-CQDs to different concentrations of Ag+ were measured and shown in Fig. S5. It is clear that the intensity of emission peak at 559 nm was progressively quenched with the enhancement of Ag+ concentration in the range of 0–200 μmol/L. When the addition of Ag+ concentration exceeded 100 μmol/L, the fluorescence intensity only decreased slightly. Thus, considering the low background signals and wide linear range, 100 μmol/L Ag+ was selected for subsequent experiments. Moreover, the fluorescence signal of the YN-CQDs with various metal ions (Hg2+, K+, Na+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Al3+) were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S6, the highest quenching efficiency of the YN-CQDs was achieved by Ag+, which is half-filled energy level because Ag+ is a transition metal that possesses d orbitals.

Since YN-CQDs exhibit good fluorescence performance within pH 5–10, the fluorescence intensities of YN-CQDs at ranges of pH 5–9.8 in the presence of Ag+ were examined. As seen in Fig. S7a and inset, the quenched fluorescence intensity of YN-CQDs in the presence of Ag+ was first enhanced and then weakened with increasing pH from 5 to 9.8. The best quenching effect was obtained when the pH value was adjusted to 7.4. These observations can be explained as follows: under acidic conditions, the carboxylic, hydroxyl, and amine groups on the surface of YN-CQDs are well protonated and thus unable to complex with Ag+. While under alkaline conditions, Ag+ is likely to hydrolyze and precipitate, which could decrease its quenching ability. Meanwhile, the influence of pH on the sensing of MZ was also examined under different pH values ranging from 5 to 9.8. As shown in Fig. S7b and inset, the maximum value of fluorescence recovery was also found at pH 7.4. Under acidic conditions, the protonation of thiol groups in MZ restricts its binding ability with Ag+, which hinders the fluorescence recovery of YN-CQDs. Under higher alkaline conditions, the interaction of numerous OH− groups with Ag+, which restricted the Ag+ and MZ interaction and limits the sensing behavior of YN-CQDs/Ag+. The above results indicate that pH 7.4 was found to be the optical pH for response of YN-CQDs towards MZ.

In order to explore the fluorescence response rates of YN-CQDs and YN-CQDs/Ag+ towards Ag+ and MZ, respectively, the influence of incubation time on the fluorescence signal of YN-CQDs/Ag+ and YN-CQDs/Ag+/MZ system were also investigated. Under the excitation wavelength of 420 nm, the fluorescence intensity of YN-CQDs reduced instantly upon the addition of Ag+ to YN-CQDs solution and flattened within 1 min (Fig. S7c). The quenched fluorescence of the YN-CQDs/Ag+ system was recovered immediately upon MZ addition and reached a steady state within 1 min, as illustrated in Fig. S7c. Therefore, in order to obtain the best experimental effect, the ideal incubation time was set at 1 min.

Under the optimized conditions described above, the sensitivity of the proposed analytical method toward MZ was studied. Mixtures of different concentrations of MZ and YN-CQDs/Ag+ solution were prepared, and their fluorescence emission responses were measured to assess the sensitivity of YN-CQDs/Ag+ system. As displayed in Fig. 2 a, the fluorescence intensity of YN-CQDs/Ag+ system solution at 559 nm was strengthened regularly with increasing amounts of MZ. In order to quantitative analysis of MZ, the relationship between MZ concentration and the recovery efficiency ((F′-F0′)/F0) were studied (Fig. 2b), where F0 was the fluorescence intensity of YN-CQDs, F0′ and F′ were the fluorescence intensities of YN-CQDs/Ag+ system in the absence and presence of MZ, respectively. When the concentration of MZ was in the range 0.003–60 μmol/L, the value of (F'-F0′)/F0 exhibited a good linear relationship versus MZ concentration (Fig. 2b inset), and the linear regression equation is (F'-F0′)/F0 = 0.0381 + 0.00795C (R2 = 0.9992). The limit of detection (LOD) for MZ was measured to be 0.8 nmol/L (signal/noise = 3). Compared with the sensing performance of other methods [1–8], the developed fluorescent probe exhibit superior features (Table S1), including no chemical modification, wider linear range and higher sensitivity for the detection of MZ, which confirms that YN-CQDs/Ag+ system possess application potential for sensing MZ.

Inspired by ultrasensitivity of this biosensor for MZ, the selectivity and competition experiments were carried out. Under the same conditions and the same concentration, some potentially coexisting substances in mouse plasma, such as glucose, sucrose, urea, L-cys, ascorbic acid, human serum albumin, and some ions (K+, Na+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Cl−, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, NO3 −, CO3 2−) were selected to explore their impact on the detection of MZ. As displayed in Fig. 3 , compared with MZ, these common biologically related substances have little influence on the fluorescence intensity of YN-CQDs/Ag+ system, while only MZ can restore the quenched fluorescence of the YN-CQDs/Ag+ system. All these results reveal that the sensing system displays excellent selectivity toward MZ detection and meets the selective requirements for biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MZ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the applicability of the fluorescence off–on probe in real complex samples, the YN-CQDs was used to determine concentrations of MZ in mouse plasma samples by standard addition method. Three levels (1.000, 2.000, and 4.000 μmol/L) of MZ were added to the mouse plasma samples, and then detected by the proposed strategy. The satisfactory recoveries of MZ were found in the range of 99.00%-102.0%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were found lower than 3.2%, these results were summarized in Table 1 .

YN-CQDs were synthesized by the one-pot hydrothermal method using extract of chlorophyll from lettuce leaf as precursor. The prepared YN-CQDs exhibited outstanding fluorescence property, good photostability, and high QY. The fluorescence of YN-CQDs can be quenched by Ag+ through static quenching. MZ recovers the fluorescence of YN-CQDs. A novel off–on fluorescence probe was established, and exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity for MZ detection.

Chunhe Yu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Dongmiao Qin: Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Xiaohua Jiang: Writing - review & editing. Xiangfei Zheng: Writing - review & editing. Biyang Deng: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21765004) and by the Guangxi Science Foundation of China (2019GXNSFAA245076). The research fund of State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources (Guangxi Normal University) (CMEMR2018-C18) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106480.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
